mission.title: Install and Configure ExternalDNS
mission.description: ExternalDNS is a Kubernetes operator that dynamically manages DNS records for Kubernetes resources like Services and Ingresses across various DNS providers. It is particularly useful for automating DNS management in cloud-native environments, allowing for seamless integration of DNS updates with Kubernetes resource changes. Key trade-offs include the need for proper permissions and configurations with the DNS provider, as well as potential latency in DNS propagation.

steps.title: Add the ExternalDNS Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the ExternalDNS Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add external-dns https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami

```

> This is the official Bitnami repository for ExternalDNS.

steps.title: Install ExternalDNS with Helm
steps.description:
This command installs ExternalDNS in the 'kube-system' namespace with the specified version and necessary configurations.

```bash

helm install external-dns external-dns/external-dns --namespace kube-system --version 0.20.0 --set provider=aws --set aws.zoneType=public --set aws.accessKey=YOUR_AWS_ACCESS_KEY --set aws.secretKey=YOUR_AWS_SECRET_KEY

```

> Replace YOUR_AWS_ACCESS_KEY and YOUR_AWS_SECRET_KEY with your actual AWS credentials.
commands: helm install external-dns external-dns/external-dns --namespace kube-system --version 0.20.0 --set provider=aws --set aws.zoneType=public --set aws.accessKey=YOUR_AWS_ACCESS_KEY --set aws.secretKey=YOUR_AWS_SECRET_KEY

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the ExternalDNS pods are running correctly in the kube-system namespace.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l app.kubernetes.io/name=external-dns

```

> You should see the ExternalDNS pod(s) in a Running state.

steps.title: Create an example Ingress resource
steps.description:
This command creates a sample Ingress resource that ExternalDNS will manage.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: example-ingress
  annotations:
    external-dns.alpha.kubernetes.io/hostname: example.yourdomain.com
spec:
  rules:
  - host: example.yourdomain.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: example-service
            port:
              number: 80
EOF

```

> Replace 'example.yourdomain.com' with your actual domain and ensure 'example-service' exists.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: example-ingress
  annotations:
    external-dns.alpha.kubernetes.io/hostname: example.yourdomain.com
spec:
  rules:
  - host: example.yourdomain.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: example-service
            port:
              number: 80
EOF

steps.title: Verify DNS records
steps.description:
This command checks if the DNS records have been created as expected.

```bash

dig example.yourdomain.com

```

> You should see the A record pointing to the LoadBalancer IP of your service.

uninstall.title: Uninstall ExternalDNS
uninstall.description:
This command removes ExternalDNS from your cluster. Be aware that DNS records managed by ExternalDNS may not be deleted automatically, leading to potential orphaned DNS entries.

```bash

helm uninstall external-dns --namespace kube-system

kubectl delete ingress example-ingress

```

upgrade.title: Upgrade ExternalDNS
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your current configuration and DNS records. After upgrading, verify that the new version is running correctly.

```bash

helm upgrade external-dns external-dns/external-dns --namespace kube-system --version 0.20.0 --set provider=aws --set aws.zoneType=public --set aws.accessKey=YOUR_AWS_ACCESS_KEY --set aws.secretKey=YOUR_AWS_SECRET_KEY

kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l app.kubernetes.io/name=external-dns

```

> Replace YOUR_AWS_ACCESS_KEY and YOUR_AWS_SECRET_KEY with your actual AWS credentials.
> Verify that the ExternalDNS pod(s) are running after the upgrade.
commands: helm upgrade external-dns external-dns/external-dns --namespace kube-system --version 0.20.0 --set provider=aws --set aws.zoneType=public --set aws.accessKey=YOUR_AWS_ACCESS_KEY --set aws.secretKey=YOUR_AWS_SECRET_KEY

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create record: InvalidChangeBatch'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This error indicates that the DNS record could not be created due to a conflict with existing records.

**Fix:**
Check for existing DNS records that may conflict and remove them if necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'No credentials found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the AWS credentials provided are incorrect or missing.

**Fix:**
Verify that the AWS access and secret keys are correctly set in the Helm install command.

troubleshooting.title: ExternalDNS pod is in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This usually happens due to misconfiguration in the provider settings or missing permissions.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the ExternalDNS pod using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kube-system' and ensure the IAM role has the necessary permissions.

troubleshooting.title: DNS records not updating
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if ExternalDNS is not able to access the Kubernetes API or if the Ingress annotations are missing.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the ExternalDNS service account has the correct permissions and that the Ingress resources have the correct annotations.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Azure DNS, improved handling of multiple zones, and enhanced logging capabilities.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the use of --domain-filter in favor of more granular zone management.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v0.15 or earlier, review the new zone management features and update your configurations accordingly.

resolution.summary: You should see the output from 'dig example.yourdomain.com' showing the correct A record pointing to your service's LoadBalancer IP.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the necessary resources and tools installed.
